content.text: Based on the official Claude documentation and multi-agent systems research,
is how agents in a multi-agent system
work together to complete complex tasks
— encompassing both how they coordinate work distribution and how they exchange information.
including parallel agent initialization, sequential agent workflows, agent communication patterns, and task delegation strategies.
How agents organize work, delegate tasks, and manage flow
How agents exchange information, share state, and pass results

Coordinator (Main Agent)
    ↓
Route to specialists
    ↓
Subagent 1 │ Subagent 2 │ Subagent 3
(isolated) │ (isolated) │ (isolated)
    ↓       ↓       ↓
All report back to coordinator only
    ↓
Coordinator synthesizes

decomposes work, delegates to subagents, aggregates results
-

never talk to each other — all coordination through coordinator
-

— subagents only report back to coordinator
-
— each subagent has own context window
: Parallel exploration tasks, independent analyses, embarrassingly parallel work

Planner Agent
    ↓
Creates specialized agents
    ↓
Team Member 1 ← ↔ → Team Member 2
    ↓          ↓          ↓
Team Member 3 ← ↔ → Team Member 4
    ↓
Shared task list + self-coordination
    ↓
Planner synthesizes

can communicate directly with each other
-

— less coordinator control, more peer autonomy
-
— teammates can message each other directly
: Complex tasks requiring discussion, collaboration, peer information sharing

Orchestrator (Central LLM)
    ↓
Analyzes task → Dynamically determines subtasks
    ↓
Subtask 1 │ Subtask 2 │ Subtask 3
Worker 1  │ Worker 2  │ Worker 3
    ↓      ↓      ↓
Results
    ↓
Orchestrator synthesizes

analyzes each unique task and dynamically determines best subtasks
-

execute assigned subtasks with specialized prompts
-

— orchestrator can revise decomposition based on what workers discover
-
— subtasks emerge as you investigate (unlike parallelization's fixed subtasks)
: Unpredictable subtasks, complex tasks, research systems requiring adaptation
Subagent returns structured output via tool result
Context passed into new API calls
Previous turn included in next request
Subagents share nothing — coordinator must explicitly pass context:

# WRONG — writer has zero context
research = run_subagent("research_agent", task="research Lambda limits")
writer = run_subagent("writer_agent", task="write about Lambda")

# RIGHT — explicitly pass context through coordinator
research = run_subagent("research_agent", task="research Lambda limits")
writer = run_subagent("writer_agent", task="write about Lambda", context=research)
How agents organize and delegate work
Who manages the flow (coordinator vs peers)
How messages are routed (one-way vs peer-to-peer)
Always deploy agents concurrently for maximum performance:

// All 3 subagents run simultaneously (same message)
[Task] description: "Security" run_in_background: true
[Task] description: "Performance" run_in_background: true
[Task] description: "Tests" run_in_background: true
2. Match Agent Specialization to Task Requirements

Assign domain-focused system prompts and tools to specialists:
- Security agent → security checklist + penetration testing tools
- Performance agent → perf benchmarks + optimization guidelines
- Documentation agent → style guide + template repository
3. Use Coordination Agents for Complex Workflows
For multi-step workflows, use coordination agents to manage dependencies.
Since each agent is stateless (only knows what's in current context window), orchestration must explicitly carry state:
Agents communicate best via structured formats:

{
  "iteration": 1,
  "score": 72,
  "pass": false,
  "critique": ["missing type hints", "no array check"],
  "suggestions": ["add @type", "check length"]
}

Define explicit message formats and routing rules:
-

When coordinating concurrent agents, handle failures explicitly:
-
: Architect (coordinator) gives tasks to electrical, plumbing, HVAC contractors who work separately and report back only to architect
: Construction crew where electricians, plumbers, and carpenters talk directly to each other ("I need to run wires before you install pipes")
: Project manager who dynamically figures out what needs to be done based on what workers discover ("You found a collapsed wall — we also need to add structural support")
Coordination = how agents organize work; Communication = how agents exchange information
Hub-and-spoke: coordinator manages flow, subagents report only to coordinator (no peer talk)
Agent Teams: shared task list, peer-to-peer messaging, self-coordination (experimental)
Orchestrator-Workers: orchestrator dynamically determines subtasks, can adapt based on results
Communication happens through context — agents don't share memory directly
Subagents share nothing — coordinator must explicitly pass context between them
Always use concurrent agent deployment for maximum performance
Match agent specialization to task requirements for optimal results
Each agent is stateless — only knows what's in current context window
Use structured outputs (JSON/XML) for clear communication between agents
Coordination patterns determine topology (hierarchical, mesh, adaptive); communication patterns determine how information flows (request-reply, pipeline, scatter-gather).
When working with coordination & communication, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with coordination & communication. Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
As your usage of coordination & communication scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
